apps.name: blackjack
apps.author: cristian-milea
apps.description: Blackjack 21 — buttons on the phone, cards on the e-ink.
apps.long_description: Single-deck blackjack. Dealer stands on all 17s. Casual mode is just Deal/Hit/Stand with a lifetime W/L/P counter. Flip 'Play for chips' to add a bank (starts at $100), bet selection, and Double Down. State persists across restarts; mid-hand RAM only.

apps.name: hello
apps.author: cristian-milea
apps.description: Minimal demo app — push text and it shows on the screen.
apps.long_description: The simplest possible pwn-app. Useful as a template when building your own. Takes a string from the phone and displays it centered on the e-ink screen.

apps.name: magic8
apps.author: cristian-milea
apps.description: Magic 8-Ball. Tap Shake for an answer.
apps.long_description: Classic Magic 8-Ball. Ask a yes/no question, tap Shake on the phone, and your fortune appears on the e-ink. The answer lives only on the e-ink — the phone is a remote.

apps.name: maze
apps.author: cristian-milea
apps.description: First-person maze crawler with random or daily-seeded mazes.
apps.long_description: Generates a fresh maze on the device (recursive backtracker) and shows it in a Wizardry-style first-person view. Phone is a D-pad (Turn-left, Forward, Back, Turn-right); the e-ink draws the perspective walls plus a fog-of-war minimap that only reveals visited cells and what you can see straight down an open corridor. Two modes: random (new seed every time) and daily (everyone on every device gets the same maze each day). Find the exit in as few steps as the optimum.

apps.name: ricochet-robots
apps.author: cristian-milea
apps.description: Ricochet Robots — slide a robot till it crashes; reach the target in the fewest moves.
apps.long_description: A single-player take on the board game Ricochet Robots. Robots slide until they crash into a wall, the arena edge, the center block, or another robot — they never stop mid-track. Land the matching shape on its target cell in as few moves as the on-device solver's optimum. Colours become four distinct drawn shapes (circle / square / triangle / diamond) for the 1-bit e-ink. Boards are generated on the device with L-shaped walls and verified solvable by a breadth-first solver; pick Easy / Medium / Hard and a board size before generating. Random mode reseeds every time; daily mode gives everyone the same board for a given size and difficulty each day.

apps.name: tide-sun
apps.author: cristian-milea
apps.description: Sunrise/sunset, moon, and a cached next-tides graph.
apps.long_description: Top strip shows today's sunrise, sunset, and the moon phase on one line, in the location's local time. Below it, a tide graph auto-fits the next three high/low tides, marking each with its time; tap Prev / Next on the phone to step through the tides, and the e-ink notes when you cross into another day. Tides come from the WorldTides API (set a free 'worldtides' API key as a secret): one sync pulls a 7-day curve for ~1 credit and caches it on the device, so the app is manual-sync — tap Sync only when the data runs low. Sunrise/sunset and moon phase are computed device-side.

secrets.label: WorldTides API key
secrets.description: Free API key from worldtides.info — powers the cached tide graph.

apps.name: vector-racing
apps.author: cristian-milea
apps.description: Turn-based vector racing. 9-way pad on the phone, circuit on the e-ink.
apps.long_description: Pen-and-paper Racetrack on the e-ink. Each turn you nudge your velocity by ±1 in x/y; momentum carries you, so brake before the corners or clip the wall. Procedurally generated closed circuits in nine flavours — oval, triangle, square, pentagon, hexagon, zigzag, scalloped, kidney, and self-crossing figure-8 — as a daily-seeded track everyone shares, or random. Pick Safe (stop on a crash) or Hardcore (back to the line) before the off, run 1–3 laps, and chase the computed optimum.

apps.name: weather
apps.author: cristian-milea
apps.long_description: Current temperature, feels-like, and weather condition plus a 12-hour precipitation bar chart. Pulls forecast data from Open-Meteo (no API key needed). Phone-side UI is just a Sync control — the e-ink is the display.
